title: Area between two curves
description: Calculate the area that's bounded by functions from above and below. In this exercise, the right and left endpoints of the area are not given.
topic_path: Finding the area between curves expressed as functions of x

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region between the graphs of $f(x)=x^2+2x+2$ and $g(x)=2x^2+3x-4$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7ce7dea188be275bf1d257e375bab81e55e83712)

From the graph, it appears that $f(x)\ge g(x)$ between the points where the graphs intersect. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{a}^{b}\left( f(x)-g(x) \right)\,dx$

where $a$ and $b$ are the $x$-coordinates of the points of intersection.

##Finding the $x$-coordinates of the intersection points

We can find the $x$-coordinate of each point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{array}{rl}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
x^2+2x+2 &=2x^2+3x-4\\\\
0&=x^2+x-6 \\\\
0&=(x+3)(x-2)
\end{array}$

The graphs intersect where $x=-3$ and $x=2$.

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{2}\left(x^2+2x+2 -(2x^2+3x-4)\right)\,dx
\\\\
&=  \displaystyle \int_{-3}^{2} \left( -x^2-x+6 \right) \,dx
\end{align}$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=} \displaystyle \int_{-3}^{2} \left( -x^2-x+6 \right) \,dx 
\\\\
&= -\dfrac{x^3}{3}-\dfrac{x^2}{2}+6x~\Bigg|_{-3}^{2}\\\\
&= \left( -\dfrac{8}{3}-2+12 \right)  - \left( 9-\dfrac{9}{2}-18 \right) \\\\
&= \dfrac{125}{6}
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $\dfrac{125}{6}$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region enclosed by the graphs of $f(x)=\sqrt{x+7}$ and $g(x)=0.5(x+7)$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8148d0c595e4d362e6d46567f09f3b9398729f50)

From the graph, it appears that $f(x)\ge g(x)$ between the points where the graphs intersect. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{a}^{b}\left( f(x)-g(x) \right)\,dx$

where $a$ and $b$ are the $x$-coordinates of the points of intersection.

##Finding the $x$-coordinates of the intersection points

We can find the $x$-coordinate of each point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
\sqrt{x+7}&= 0.5(x+7)\\\\
x+7 &= 0.25(x+7)^2 \\\\
0 &=0.25(x+7)^2 - (x+7) \\\\
0 &= (x+7)(0.25(x+7)-1) \\
0 &= (x+7)(0.25x+0.75)
\end{align}$

We have two possible solutions at $x=\greenD{-7}$ and $x=\goldE{-3}$ .  Because this was a radical equation, let's check for extraneous solutions by plugging our possible solutions back into the original equation.

$\begin{align}
\sqrt{\greenD{-7}+7}&\stackrel{?}=0.5(\greenD{-7}+7)\\\\
0& \stackrel{\checkmark}= 0
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\sqrt{\goldE{-3}+7}&\stackrel{?}=0.5(\goldE{-3}+7)\\\\
2& \stackrel{\checkmark}= 2
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect where $x=-7$ and $x=-3$ .

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\displaystyle \int_{-7}^{-3}\left(\sqrt{x+7}-(0.5(x+7))\right)\,dx$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{-7}^{-3} \left( \sqrt{x+7}- (0.5(x+7)) \right) \,dx
\\\\
&= \dfrac{2}{3}(x+7)^{3/2}-\dfrac14x^2-\dfrac72x~\Bigg|_{-7}^{-3} \\\\
&= \left( \dfrac{16}{3}-\dfrac{9}{4}+\dfrac{21}{2} \right) - \left(  0 - \dfrac{49}{4}+\dfrac{49}{2}\right) \\\\
&= \dfrac{4}{3}
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $ \dfrac{4}{3}$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region between the graphs of $f(x)=x^2+2x$ and $g(x)=2x+1$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/16485f412fc55840e7bb4c95ebaec13e594acf44)

From the graph, it appears that $g(x)\ge f(x)$ between the points where the graphs intersect. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{a}^{b}\left( g(x)-f(x) \right)\,dx$

where $a$ and $b$ are the $x$-coordinates of the points of intersection.

##Finding the $x$-coordinates of the intersection points

We can find the $x$-coordinate of each point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
x^2+2x &=2x+1\\\\
x^2 &= 1 \\\\
x &= \pm 1
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect where $x=-1$ and $x=1$.

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{-1}^{1}\left(2x+1-(x^2+2x)\right)\,dx
\\\\
&= \int_{-1}^{1}\left(1-x^2\right)\,dx
\end{align}$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{-1}^{1} \left( 1- x^2 \right) \,dx
\\\\
&= x - \dfrac{x^3}{3}~\Bigg|_{-1}^{1} \\\\
&= \left( 1-\dfrac{1}{3} \right) - \left( -1+\dfrac{1}{3} \right) \\\\
&= \dfrac{4}{3}
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $ \dfrac{4}{3}$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region between the graphs of $f(x)=3x^2+3x-8$ and $g(x)=2x^2+x+7$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/50cd9968e015944eec7d59ab16b65fa91491f06a)

From the graph, it appears that $g(x)\ge f(x)$ between the points where the graphs intersect. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{a}^{b}\left( g(x)-f(x) \right)\,dx$

where $a$ and $b$ are the $x$-coordinates of the points of intersection.

##Finding the $x$-coordinates of the intersection points

We can find the $x$-coordinate of each point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
3x^2+3x-8&=2x^2+x+7\\\\
x^2+2x-15 &= 0\\\\
(x+5)(x-3)&= 0
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect where $x=-5$ and $x=3$.

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{3}\left(2x^2+x+7-(3x^2+3x-8)\right)\,dx
\\\\
&= \int_{-5}^{3}\left(-x^2-2x+15\right)\,dx
\end{align}$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{3}\left(-x^2-2x+15\right) \,dx
\\\\
&= -\dfrac{x^3}{3}-x^2+15x~\Bigg|_{-5}^{3} \\\\
&= \left( -9-9+45 \right) - \left( \dfrac{125}{3} -25 -75\right) \\\\
&= \dfrac{256}{3}
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $\dfrac{256}{3}$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region enclosed by the graphs of $f(x)=x^2+2x+11$ , $g(x)=-4x+2$ , and $x=0$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d4416e170df7058bd072f6151ed67721b6116ec)

From the graph, it appears that $f(x)\ge g(x)$ between the point where the graphs intersect and $x=0$. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{a}^{0}\left( f(x)-g(x) \right)\,dx$

where $a$ is the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection.

##Finding the $x$-coordinate of the intersection point

We can find the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
x^2+2x+11 &=-4x+2\\\\
x^2+6x+9 &= 0 \\\\
(x+3)^2 &=0
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect where $x=-3$ .

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{0}\left(x^2+2x+11 -(-4x+2)\right)\,dx
\\\\
&= \int_{-3}^{0} \left( x^2+6x+9 \right) \,dx
\end{align}$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=} \displaystyle \int_{-3}^{0} \left( x^2+6x+9 \right) \,dx
\\\\
&= \dfrac{x^3}{3} + 3x^2 + 9x~\Bigg|_{-3}^{0} \\\\
&= \left( 0+0+0 \right) - \left( -9+27-27 \right)  \\\\
&=9
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $9$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region bound by the graphs of $f(x)=\sqrt{x-2}$ , $g(x)=14-x$ , and $x=2$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8a216f45fb04dc2a852217a9a422d263aefd7af6)

From the graph, it appears that $g(x)\ge f(x)$ between $x=2$ and the point where the graphs intersect. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{2}^{b}\left( g(x)-f(x) \right)\,dx$

where $b$ is the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection.

##Finding the $x$-coordinate of the intersection point

We can find the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
\sqrt{x-2}&=14-x\\\\
x-2 &=x^2-28x+196 \\\\
0 &= x^2-29x+198 \\\\
0 &= (x-11) (x-18)
\end{align}$

We have two possible solutions at $x=\greenD{11}$ and $x=\goldE{18}$ .  Because this was a radical equation, let's check for extraneous solutions by plugging our possible solutions back into the original equation.

$\begin{align}
\sqrt{\greenD{11}-2}&\stackrel{?}=14-\greenD{11}\\\\
3 & \stackrel{\checkmark}= 3
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\sqrt{\goldE{18}-2}&\stackrel{?}=14-\goldE{18}\\\\
4 & \neq -4
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect where $x=11$.

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\displaystyle \int_{2}^{11}\left(14-x-(\sqrt{x-2})\right)\,dx$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{2}^{11} \left( 14-x- (\sqrt{x-2}) \right) \,dx
\\\\
&= 14x-\dfrac{x^2}{2}-\dfrac{2(x-2)^{3/2}}{3}~\Bigg|_{2}^{11} \\\\
&= \left( 154-\dfrac{121}{2}-18 \right) - \left( 28-2-0 \right) \\\\
&= \dfrac{99}{2}
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $\dfrac{99}{2}$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region bound by the graphs of $f(x)=x^2+3$, $g(x)=2x+6$, and $x=0$ in quadrant $\text{I}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2bc03313daee98b1f388e55d0eb047fd3cbfba20)

From the graph, it appears that $g(x)\ge f(x)$ between the points where the graphs intersect. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{b}\left( g(x)-f(x) \right)\,dx$

where $b$ is the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection that falls in quadrant $\text{I}$.

##Finding the $x$-coordinates of the intersection points

We can find the $x$-coordinate of each point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
x^2+3&=2x+6\\\\
x^2-2x-3 &= 0 \\\\
(x-3)(x+1) &=0
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect where $x=-1$ and $x=3$.  Because we only want the part of the graph in quadrant $\text{I}$ , we will use $x=3$ as our upper bound.

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{0}^{3}\left(2x+6-(x^2+3)\right)\,dx
\\\\
&= \int_{0}^{3}\left(-x^2+2x+3\right)\,dx
\end{align}$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{0}^{3}\left(-x^2+2x+3\right)\,dx
\\\\
&= -\dfrac{x^3}{3}+x^2+3x~\Bigg|_{0}^{3} \\\\
&= \left( -9+9+9 \right) - \left( 0+0+0 \right) \\\\
&= 9
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $9$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region bound by the graphs of $f(x)=-x^2$ , $g(x)=3x-10$, and $x=0$ in quadrant $\text{IV}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/80de3fa17f494b5edfb1a1feaa61d29f76da2de5)

From the graph, it appears that $f(x)\ge g(x)$ between the points where the graphs intersect. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{b}\left( f(x)-g(x) \right)\,dx$

where $b$ is the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection in quadrant $\text{IV}$.

##Finding the $x$-coordinates of the intersection points

We can find the $x$-coordinate of each point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
-x^2&=3x-10\\\\
0 &= x^2+3x-10 \\\\
0 &= (x+5)(x-2)
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect where $x=-5$ and $x=2$. Because we only want the part of the graph in quadrant $\text{I}V$ , we will use $x=2$ as our upper bound.

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{0}^{2}\left(-x^2-(3x-10)\right)\,dx
\\\\
&= \int_{0}^{2}\left(-x^2-3x+10\right)\,dx
\end{align}$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{0}^{2} \left( -x^2- 3x+10 \right) \,dx
\\\\
&= -\dfrac{x^3}{3}-\dfrac{3x^2}{2}+10x~\Bigg|_{0}^{2} \\\\
&= \left( -\dfrac{8}{3}-6+20 \right) - \left( 0-0+0 \right) \\\\
&= \dfrac{34}{3}
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $\dfrac{34}{3}$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region between the graphs of $f(x)=\dfrac{4}{x}$ , $g(x)=4$, and $x=4$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e03673ea335eaf54cd26b5e53da18016b98f5e74)

From the graph, it appears that $g(x)\ge f(x)$ between the point where the graphs intersect and $x=4$. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{a}^{4}\left( g(x)-f(x) \right)\,dx$

where $a$ is the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection.

##Finding the $x$-coordinate of the intersection point

We can find the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
\dfrac{4}{x}&=4\\\\
4 &=4x\\\\
1 &=x
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect where $x=1$ .

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\displaystyle \int_{1}^{4}\left(4-\left(\dfrac{4}{x}\right)\right)\,dx$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{1}^{4} \left( 4- \left(\dfrac{4}{x} \right) \right) \,dx
\\\\
&= 4x-4\ln|x|~\Bigg|_{1}^{4} \\\\
&= \left( 16-4\ln|4| \right) - \left( 4-0 \right) \\\\
&= 12-4\ln(4)
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $12-4\ln(4)$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region between $2$ consecutive points where the graphs of $f(x)=\cos(x)$ and $g(x)=-\cos(x)+2$ intersect?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9156622d5e1d95964daa139bf46d7bed0253ca66)

From the graph, it appears that $g(x)\ge f(x)$ between the points where the graphs intersect. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{a}^{b}\left( g(x)-f(x) \right)\,dx$

where $a$ and $b$ are the $x$-coordinates of two consecutive points of intersection.

##Finding the $x$-coordinates of the intersection points

We can find the $x$-coordinate of each point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
\cos(x)&=-\cos(x)+2\\\\
2\cos(x) &= 2 \\\\
\cos(x) &=1
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect when $x=0\pm 2k\pi$ , where $k$ is any integer.  Since we want $2$ consecutive values, let's use $k=0$ and $k=1$, which means we will use the points $x=0$ and $x=2\pi$ .

*Note: The area between the graphs is the same between any $2$ consecutive points where they meet, so it does not matter which values we picked for $k$, as long as they were consecutive.*

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{0}^{2\pi}\left(-\cos(x)+2-(\cos(x))\right)\,dx
\\\\
&= \int_{0}^{2\pi}\left(-2\cos(x)+2\right)\,dx
\end{align}$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{0}^{2\pi}\left(-2\cos(x)+2\right)\,dx
\\\\
&= -2\sin(x)+2x~\Bigg|_{0}^{2\pi} \\\\
&= \left( 0+4\pi \right) - \left( 0+0 \right) \\\\
&= 4\pi
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $4\pi$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graphs of the functions $f(x)=\sin(x)$ and $g(x)=\dfrac{1}{2}$ intersect at $2$ points on the interval $0 < x < \pi$ .

**What is the area of the region bound by the graphs of $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ between those points of intersection ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9dd5f8966da60b39754c252092f4839f801acd31)

From the graph, it appears that $f(x)\ge g(x)$ between the points where the graphs intersect. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{a}^{b}\left( f(x)-g(x) \right)\,dx$

where $a$ and $b$ are the $x$-coordinates of the points of intersection.

##Finding the $x$-coordinates of the intersection points

We can find the $x$-coordinate of each point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
\sin(x)&=\dfrac{1}{2}
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect when $x=\dfrac{\pi}{6}\pm 2k\pi$ and when $x=\dfrac{5\pi}{6} \pm 2k\pi$ , where $k$ is any integer.  Since we want values of $x$ on the interval $0 < x < \pi$, we will use $x=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ and $x= \dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ .

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\displaystyle \int_{\pi/6}^{5\pi/6}\left(\sin(x)-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)\,dx$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{\pi/6}^{5\pi/6} \left( \sin(x)- \dfrac{1}{2} \right) \,dx
\\\\
&=-\cos(x)-\dfrac{x}{2}~ \Bigg|_{\pi/6}^{5\pi/6} \\\\
&= \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} -\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) - \left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\dfrac{\pi}{12}  \right) \\\\
&= \sqrt{3}-\dfrac{\pi}{3}
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $\sqrt{3}-\dfrac{\pi}{3}$ square units.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the area of the region bound by the graphs of $f(x)=4$ , $g(x)=\dfrac{e^x}{5}$ , and $x=0$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Visualizing the area

We sketch the graphs of $f$ and $g$ first.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/013dc18f4cce4ab4bcb37f4e71b0d05882efe7d6)

From the graph, it appears that $f(x)\ge g(x)$ between $x=0$ and the point where the graphs intersect. From this we are looking to evaluate:

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{b}\left( f(x)-g(x) \right)\,dx$

where $b$ is the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection.

##Finding the $x$-coordinate of the intersection point

We can find the $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection by setting the functions equal to each other and solving the resulting equation.

$\begin{align}
f(x)&=g(x) \\\\
4&=\dfrac{e^x}{5}\\\\
20 &= e^x \\\\
\ln (20) &= x
\end{align}$

The graphs intersect where $x=\ln(20)$.

##Setting up the definite integral

Thus, the area of the shaded region pictured above is given by:

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{\ln(20)}\left(4-\dfrac{e^x}{5}\right)\,dx$

##Evaluating the definite integral

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle \int_{0}^{\ln(20)} \left( \dfrac{e^x}{5}- (4) \right) \,dx
\\\\
&= 4x-\dfrac{e^x}{5}~\Bigg|_{0}^{\ln(20)} \\\\
&= \left(4\ln(20) -4  \right) - \left( \dfrac{1}{5} -0\right)\\\\
&= 4\ln(20) - \dfrac{19}{5} 
\end{align}$

##Answer

The area is $4\ln(20) - \dfrac{19}{5} $ square units.
